speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we were-- you were telling us the story about the Ku Klux Klan.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh, one fine day, this man who was the chief intelligence officer of the Ku Klux Klan told me that there is a plan to have these little Klan figures on the desk of the major bosses of the studios. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Take-- you can take your hand-- take your hand off the front. Yeah, that's better.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you get it? Here, you need to remove-- here, let me show you. Just hold it like that so that we can see the K-I-G-Y. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I called Mendel Silberberg, whom I mentioned to you before is the leader of the group, and told him about it. No, I didn't call him. I went to see him. We were in the same building.

speaker: interviewer
text: He reached for the-- he reached for his telephone. I say, what are you going to do, Mendel? He says, I want to tell Louis B. Mayer, and Harry Cohen, and Jack-- well, don't you do that. Don't you do that. That would indicate to them-- to the Kluxes-- that would indicate to the Kluxes-- that there is a leak. So fortunately, I stopped him from-- from calling them. How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: But--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- how did you manage to get the confidence--

speaker: interviewee
text: I asked--

speaker: interviewer
text: --of this man from the Ku Klux Klan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Originally, he was recommended to us by Naval Intelligence. He operated a taxi, a boat taxi, from Long Beach over to Catalina. And Naval Intelligence recommended him-- also recommended the only woman I had as an undercover operator. She was recommended by Naval Intelligence. Her father was a high ranking naval officer and got himself into a jam. And he was fired. And she had to support her mother. So the other naval officers, being-- having been friends of the fired naval officer, recommended him to me. I've worked closely primarily with Captain Eli Zacharias, who did a book about his experiences as a Naval Intelligence officer, who, incidentally, was not made a captain for a long time-- should have been made a captain earlier. He was not promoted as quickly as I and he thought he should have been--

speaker: interviewer
text: This--

speaker: interviewee
text: --because, unfortunately for him, he was a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: This woman you mentioned, who you said was--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was terrific.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she covered especially the German house. I could have three men there and her, none of them knowing who they really were. And her six- or eight-page report gave me more valuable information than those three men. She--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what were in the reports, what she wrote about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was primarily what was going on in a German house, what was said. For instance, there was a woman by the real name of Pakita Shishmarova. Write it any way you want to-- Pakita Shishmarova. She was the widow of a high-ranking naval officer in the Czarist period. She went under the name of Leslie Fry. You couldn't tell by the word Leslie whether it is a male or female. She is the one who brought the which I do not need to describe now. If anybody who doesn't know it should find out. She brought the which this is a plot of Jews, having met in a Jewish cemetery in Prague-- 'Prayg,' you would say in Czechoslovakia-- a plot to gain control of the whole world. she brings them to the United States, and meets a man by name of Henry Ford, who prints them in And these were used against Jews all over the world, from the day they were invented. And to describe how they were invented would take far too long-- until now. She was more important. She had more power than this fellow Schwinn, who spoke in Chicago, who was the leader-- then, the leader of the Bund here. She ordered him around. She was a paid agent. It was a man by the name of Henry Allen who was an employee of hers-- paid with German money. He was a great rabble rouser. He was fantastic-- very, very able. And he delivered the speeches. But he didn't like her and he didn't trust her. I told my agents never to steal a little piece of paper, even the opportunity is great. Because a man can figure out the people was here, so and so was here, ergo, he must have stolen it. Well, this guy stole from his employer, from Mrs. Fry. And he carried with him, in a big briefcase, this evidence that she is a Nazi agent. Mrs. Fry decides to use this man to go to the highest building on Hollywood Boulevard, near Vine Street-- go to the top of it, and throw from the top some pro-Nazi, anti-Semitic, anti-Jewish movie pamphlets. This man did that. We knew it would happen because the same man who was the expert on the Klan was with him. A few days later, I get the report from [INAUDIBLE] that they are going to do the same thing in San Diego. My man was to go with them. I call Zacharias. I told Zacharias earlier that he carries this big briefcase. And in it Allen, Henry Allen, has evidence that Fry is a-- a paid Nazi agent. San Diego. Handbills thrown down. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Both Allen and my man are arrested. The big-- briefcase is taken and rushed over to Naval headquarters, with the police, going real fast, driving over there. And they-- they got hold of a lawyer right away-- and a pro-Nazi and anti-Jewish son of a so-and-so. They knew, ah ha, they are after the briefcase. So they immediately moved what needed to be moved, as a good lawyer, to get the briefcase back. My guy gets on the telephone, pretends to-- to the others that he is calling his wife, and says, we are sprung. We are out. We are on our way back and we have got the briefcase.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on the way back, they were arrested a second time by the police, to give them enough time to then photostat-- it was a slow process. You had no Xerox there-- to photostat the rest of it, and then came home. It was quite exciting.

speaker: interviewee
text: But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] somebody had to swear out a complaint. Buron Fitts was then the District Attorney. He had a man in charge of complaints by the name of Johnny Klein-- not related to my mother. My mother's name was Klein. Got the complaint signed by the only one who was available, me. One day, I paid for it. Came home. Two big bums rushed at me, beat me up. Fortunately, someone came up the street and saw that-- saw what was going on. But they saw that woman coming and they ran. So I wasn't hurt badly. But that's the price I paid. And I don't mind it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my wife said, what happened, what happened? I say, I didn't see the step and I broke my glasses. She still doesn't know. Perhaps you could talk to us about-- perhaps you could talk to us about La Crescenta Park and what happened there.

speaker: interviewee
text: About what?

speaker: interviewer
text: La Crescenta? The park?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. What park?

speaker: interviewer
text: Hindenburg-- it became Hindenburg Park.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Hindenburg Park. Well they-- at Hindenburg Park, which was in one of the outlying areas, they had picnics. And they had marched around. They loved to march, even without music. And lots of speechifying, lots of talks about the Fatherland, the greatest country in the world, lots of angry, ugly stuff about the Jews, and the Communists, and the Socialists, and so on and so forth. The German consul-- the early German consul was not pro-Nazi, but they-- they canned him. And then at one after the other consuls were as bad as the worst of the Nazis-- hate mongers. Hate mongers, all of them, each one of them. But there was something similar-- in those people that reminds you of what we see now in the people who go and blow up a building.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were going to tell me-- are you-- are you feeling OK? Do you need to take a--

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you feeling all right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I'm all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. But I was asking you about Hindenburg Park.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to tell me anything else about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was just good picnicking and a lot of singing of German songs-- best of them [? waltz. ?] Interesting about it-- it comes to me now. It never occurred to me before-- interesting to me now is the song they sang most frequently was-- I'll translate-- 'I had a comrade. A better one you cannot find. A bullet came flying'-- and then the rest of it goes, crying over the thing. That this song was more than the typical song that a bunch of Americans would want to sing together on picnic grounds. The glorification of fighting-- fighting for the Fatherland. It reminds you again of what I told you earlier about what happened when the revolution broke out in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever attended any of these events in--

speaker: interviewee
text: At Hindenburg Park, yes, but it didn't mean very much. It was the speechifying-- what I call it, speechifying, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they get people to come and listen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, these were mostly members of the Bund. And they were supposed to bring friends, in order to convince the friends that they, too, should become good Nazis. What it was, really-- that's [INAUDIBLE] objective, to develop real comradeship among them. But it-- and yet, you know, there was a lot of distrust of each other. Once they appointed committees of three. Everybody-- practically everybody-- was on the committee to investigate somebody. So I told my guys, for God's sake, watch. If you are appointed to come and investigate somebody, somebody else must have been appointed to-- to investigate you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about do you know anything about the possibility of some sort of Bund that was in Rustic Canyon, any kind of undercover work that went on there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall it. It may have been, but it doesn't strike a note in my memory.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard-- I heard something like it, but I don't know what it really was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after-- after the Sudetenland was taken, and after-- and once the war began, where did that put you in your organization? What were you doing by this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, remember, it was in 1938 that I left the motion picture business and went to work for the Jewish community as the head of the Community Relations Committee. And I was with them until about 1970-- yeah, about 25 years ago. And after the-- I'll jump now, and then I'll come back. After the war was over, I continued my work, more in order to gain acceptance not only of Jews, but also of blacks, Hispanics-- at that time, you didn't say Hispanics. You used-- it's Mexicans-- and so on and so forth. I did a lot of work in-- in-- in that field.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed with them until 1970, when I took an early retirement, because I would have to retire at age 65. I was then 62. I said to myself, I better quit now, before I'm too old to ever gain another job, you know, by waiting until I'm 65. So I left there in 1970.

speaker: interviewee
text: I served on its Executive Committee. I [INAUDIBLE] Vice-Chairman. I don't-- I continued doing a lot of work with them and for them and I'm doing it even now, but not so much just looking out for the goodwill, or the favor and the health of Jews, but all people. For instance, I argued early, when I came to work for the Community Relations Committee, this must not be a Jewish society for-- for prevention of cruelty to Jews. This must fight for decency and acceptance of every human being, and every good American. And I had some people who disagreed with me. But I won that battle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now we're going to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Are we on air?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: The question is, as I understand it, how did I receive the information? Two-- no, I'll start it differently. I did bel-- I believe I told you that we published a weekly newsletter, which reached millions of people. In order to have good information, I read two German language newspapers. One was the newspaper You don't need to put that down. which was the official organ of the Nazi party. The other one was 'Angriff' means 'attack.' was published by that great-- great, really wonderful, very capable and competent-- propaganda minister, Dr. Paul Joseph Goebbels. By reading this, I was able to see what was cooking. Not that I could predict every little thing, but when you see all of the sudden constant criticism of the government of Poland for allegedly mistreating Germans living in Poland-- because a certain part of Poland originally was German. So they found them there. So you see this. And then you say to yourself, ah ha, this is Hitler's next move. And I was right. And that's how I came about, pointing at this one thing. The other thing is, until Czechoslovakia was sold down the river at Munich, the Socialists and Social Democrats operated out of Czechoslovakia or the Prague. Some people say 'Prayg'-- P-R-A-G-U-E. They published quite a number of-- you can't really call them brochures. It was a soft-covered, 40 or 50 pages, on very thin, thin paper. You had to have things small in order to smuggle them into Germany. You also had the other things that you-- they stuck into matchboxes, you know. My sister carried matchboxes into Germany. You open it up and you don't find matches in there, but the typical-- the box where we-- an exact copy of the matchboxes you had inside Germany. You opened it up. Also little envelopes, in which you would find seeds-- you know, to plant flowers, to have flowers, even though [INAUDIBLE] well, I've dropped here, dropped here, very carefully. But I'm sorry that I am sitting here. I have some of those brochures in there, if we are [INAUDIBLE] I'll grab a hold of it in order to show them to you. But talking about the criticism of Poland-- the attack on Poles by the Germans-- I deliberately brought this in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how did you come to get this book?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a human being by the name of Bruno Luecke. Bruno-- you know how to spell. Luecke-- L-U-E-C-K-E. Very typical German name. He is the one who subscribed to the two newspapers I mentioned just a minute ago. There was, in the city of Erfurt-- E-R-F-U-R-T-- in the city of Erfurt, a man who published-- a man by the name of Colonel Fleischauer. Meat cutter-- Fleischauer. He printed a monthly bulletin in seven languages, including-- and one of them being English, which were sent all over the world, you know, to South America, Asia, all over-- all Nazi propaganda. And this-- this horrible guy lists-- listed on the front cover of that newsletter all the good, reliable Nazis all over the world. So if I saw John Jones in-- in South Africa, is a-- no. No, I said it the wrong way. When Bruno Luecke saw that John Jones in South Africa is a good Nazi, Bruno Luecke began corresponding with him, changing information. What he read here, he passed onto him there, vice versa-- all over. And out of responses he got-- they exchanged information in order to be better fighters for the cause. Out of that, I got an awful lot of information. Because Bruno Luecke didn't exist. The post office box.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were Bruno Luecke.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was Bruno Luecke. I killed him. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you kill him?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- when the war was over. When the war was over. Interesting thing-- you know, the FBI, they came to my office. Practically every day somebody from the FBI was there wanting to know if-- some of them became good friends. I said, for God's sake, will you please lay off of my post office box? I'll give it to you. [LAUGHS] I had my fun. Yeah, I wanted to say something about that. This has German ugly deeds. This is worse than ugly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cruel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cruel. Cruel is the word.

speaker: interviewer
text: You need to hold the book up for us, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was on-- or Bruno Luecke was on the mailing list. This is what made me tell that story. This arrived about two weeks after the attack on Poland, which was the beginning of the war, which means it must have been prepared way in advance of the actual attack on [INAUDIBLE]. Now, it describes how horrible the poor Germans were treated there. And then, pages and pages of German-- of Polish-- Polish deeds-- cruel deeds, all of this within two weeks after the beginning of the war. They printed a smaller copy of it-- maybe no more than 40 or 50 pages-- of a little bit of text and a lot of-- and some of those pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a picture you showed me, which was very difficult to look at, much earlier.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they-- they are, each one of them. Of course, these were maybe even actors, posing as if they're lying there dead. And here, Posen was one of the largest city in Eastern Germany. Graves above graves, meaning more graves and more graves [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Now this was published in Posen, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is a reprint of an article that appeared in the or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which you received--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah,

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you receive this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one?

speaker: interviewer
text: Or did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Same source. I mean, most of the stuff I got out of Germany, but also out of Czechoslovakia, as long as it was ours, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your sister involved with helping you at all in Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was-- I mean, if you would meet her at her younger age-- she's dead now-- and if you would meet her, you would say, oh, this is a typical German girl that Hitler would love-- very Aryan looking. So she was primarily a courier. She carried those matchboxes. She carried the seed, envelopes, and so on, so forth. She carried money sometimes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- how long did your family remain in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got them out-- out of Czech-- I've got to do it back side forward. We got them into-- out of Czechoslovakia about two weeks before the war began. That would have been in 1939. They were in Czechoslovakia probably about three-- three or four years, out of-- from Berlin into Czechoslovakia. My mother had a sister in Czechoslovakia, so in-- in Bruenn. B-R-U-E- double N. The Czechs called it the 'Brnn.' No vowel in it. B-R- double N, 'Brnn.'

speaker: interviewer
text: What made them leave Germany to begin with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Nazi-- the Nazi situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they manage to even get papers to leave Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't get any papers. My kid brother smuggled them. They called them the 'Green Border'-- went over the mountains, first into the Bavaria, then across into Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then how did you manage to get them out of Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a very long story. Do you mind a long story but interesting story? You know a  man by the name of President-- President Wilson? He had an adviser, whose name escapes me, but it'll come to me before I'm finished this story. He had an advisor, but let's identify him only as an advisor. House-- Colonel Mandell House, H-O-U-S-E, like a house. He was the expert on international affairs. When a person was appointed to be an ambassador to any country, the proper thing for him was to first call on Colonel House, who would give you-- would give him, her advice. And the end of this discussion always was, keep me informed. Keep me informed. I want to know. I want to know. I have to know, because the president relies on me-- not that they put it that way, but that was the meaning of it. And he had good information. Colonel House died. [SIGHS] There was a man by name-- by the name of James W. Gerard-- G-E-R-A-R-D, James W. Gerard, the famous Gerard family, which owned half of the copper mines in Chile, an attorney in New York. He took over that network of correspondents. You know, people would report to him. And he would write to them. He would tell them, do this, and don't do that. James W. Gerard was the-- before that, James W. Gerard was the American ambassador to Germany, during the Kaiser's period. My grandfather was an American citizen. Once we got into World War I, he was an enemy alien. My grandfather and his family, especially older ones, and Gerard, were close like this. When I came to America, the first thing I did, before moving on to Chicago, where I had a lot of relatives-- call on Uncle Jimmy. To him, he was-- for me, he was Uncle Jimmy, not Ambassador James W. Gerard. Gerard maintained that House network, got in touch with the ambassador in Italy. The ambassador-- our ambassador then. The ambassador in Italy spoke to Count Ciano-- C-I-A-N-O, Count Ciano, Mussolini's son-in-law, who was Mussolini's Secretary of Foreign Affairs. The Secretary of Foreign Affairs, Count Ciano, spoke to Himmler-- and you know who Himmler was. And Himmler approved [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] his doing what needed to be done to let him out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how you got--

speaker: interviewee
text: It also cost money. I borrowed money. And I was in debt for many-- long, long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're going to change the reel now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
